caveats: a: No labor-compensation ratio is available for this industry.
a: Public occupation evidence is not feedlot- or custom-feeding-specific.
rho: Bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
rho: Animal-welfare and environmental accountability constrain unattended automation.
e: The direct custom-feeding evidence is from the 2011 Feedlot study.
e: Feedlot, establishment, cattle owner, and operating company are not equivalent.
e: The supplied firm count is missing and must not be inferred.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: No denominator-based control-transfer series for independent custom feedlots.
q: No public contract panel quantifies pass-through.
q: Retention differs between fixed yardage, cost-plus feed, and gain- or performance-linked arrangements.
d5: Monthly inventory is a weak five-year predictor.
d5: Aggregate fed-cattle volume does not equal independent custom-feedlot service demand.
o: Operator identity may shift toward larger integrated firms even if physical feeding persists.
o: This primitive excludes labor automation already reflected in a and rho.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring production contracts with cattle owners, typically combining per-head or per-day yardage, feed and input charges or markups, animal-health charges, and performance reporting; cattle may be marketed to packers for the owner.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 112112 mixes fee-based custom feeding with principal-risk cattle ownership. The lens keeps custom-feeding operators because they provide a repeat outsourced service and excludes own-cattle-only production so one service-business screen remains coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Feedlots that only own and trade their cattle, packer-captive or vertically integrated lots, cow-calf and backgrounding operations, passive facilities, hobby farms, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM cattle feedlots that repeatedly provide custom finishing and related feeding, animal-care, recordkeeping, and marketing-support services for cattle owned by external ranchers, producers, or investors.

a.bridge: BLS describes animal farmworkers and agricultural managers across animal production rather than custom cattle feedlots. I map ration and intake analysis, health-alert triage, cattle and pen records, feed procurement, scheduling, billing, compliance documentation, and marketing support as exposed; feeding, pen cleaning, animal handling, equipment operation, maintenance, and direct health treatment remain physical. The broad proxy may overstate exposure where small operators combine management and manual work.
caveats: No labor-compensation ratio is available for this industry.
Public occupation evidence is not feedlot- or custom-feeding-specific.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries and software-usage logs from custom feedlots of different capacities, including time spent on feeding, health, compliance, procurement, billing, and customer reporting, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: Data-intensive monitoring and administration offer a real but bounded opportunity because continuous animal care, feed delivery, manure handling, and facility work dominate operations.

d5.bridge: USDA NASS reports 11.682 million cattle on feed in feedlots with capacity of at least 1,000 head on June 1, 2026, 2 percent above a year earlier, while USDA ERS lowered 2026 and 2027 beef-production forecasts by less than 1 percent because fed-cattle slaughter was slower. I translate these near-term inventory and production measures into broadly stable five-year constant-quality demand for custom finishing, with uncertainty from herd rebuilding, imports, beef prices, consumer substitution, capacity concentration, and cattle cycles. The proxy may overstate demand for independent LMM custom lots if volume consolidates into very large or captive operations.
caveats: Monthly inventory is a weak five-year predictor.
Aggregate fed-cattle volume does not equal independent custom-feedlot service demand.
d5.change_evidence: USDA baseline projections for cattle placements and beef consumption, feedlot-capacity closures and additions, custom-fed shares, packer ownership, and five-year customer retention cohorts would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: Cattle finishing remains necessary for grain-finished beef, but the cattle cycle and continued concentration make growth uncertain even when aggregate beef demand is durable.

e.bridge: USDA APHIS reported that 50.2 percent of surveyed feedlots custom-fed some cattle for other owners, including 38.0 percent of feedlots with capacity from 1,000 to 7,999 head and 79.9 percent at 8,000 head or more. I use this dated capacity-based incidence as the center, then discount partial custom activity, own-cattle-only operations, captive integration, firms outside the EBITDA band, and nontransferable owner-dependent operations. The old survey may understate current custom-feeding prevalence at scaled lots but overstate firms whose service activity is substantial enough for the lens.
caveats: The direct custom-feeding evidence is from the 2011 Feedlot study.
Feedlot, establishment, cattle owner, and operating company are not equivalent.
The supplied firm count is missing and must not be inferred.
e.change_evidence: A current USDA NAHMS or ARMS feedlot survey linked to custom-fed revenue share, ownership, independence, normalized EBITDA, and customer concentration would establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Custom feeding is a recognized business model but not universal, and merely feeding some outside cattle does not establish that the firm is an eligible recurring service platform.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Operator identity may shift toward larger integrated firms even if physical feeding persists.
This primitive excludes labor automation already reflected in a and rho.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread ranch self-finishing, packer vertical integration displacing custom lots, autonomous facilities operating without accountable local management, or structural substitution away from fed beef would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires an accountable operator to receive, feed, monitor, treat, handle, and ship live cattle while managing people, equipment, manure, permits, and customer funds. Software can improve decisions but does not replace custody and facility responsibility.

caveats: No public contract panel quantifies pass-through.
Retention differs between fixed yardage, cost-plus feed, and gain- or performance-linked arrangements.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of custom-feeding contracts, yardage and feed-markup histories, renewal repricing, performance guarantees, and customer interviews would directly test retained benefit.
q.rationale: Operators can initially retain administrative, feed-efficiency, and health-management gains through yardage and service margins. Itemized feed charges, negotiated markups, performance comparisons, contract renewals, cattle-owner sophistication, and competition among feedlots pass a meaningful share back to customers.

caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
Animal-welfare and environmental accountability constrain unattended automation.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-site trials reporting implemented labor hours, false-positive health alerts, feed conversion, morbidity, mortality, billing accuracy, and compliance time over complete feeding cycles would move the range.
rho.rationale: Ration support, computer-vision triage, anomaly detection, forecasting, scheduling, records, and billing can be implemented within five years, but fragmented sensor data, false animal-health alerts, biosecurity, environmental compliance, equipment integration, rural connectivity, and thin management teams limit realization.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve evidence covers small employer firms generally rather than feedlots. I translate older-owner succession pressure into a five-year control-transfer probability and discount family succession, cattle or land sales without an operating-company transfer, closures, internal reorganizations, and sales to integrated strategic owners outside the lens. Asset intensity and consolidation could raise transaction incidence, while family ownership could lower qualifying transfers.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
No denominator-based control-transfer series for independent custom feedlots.
s5.change_evidence: Feedlot broker records, USDA operator-age and succession tables, lender workout data, state CAFO permit ownership changes, and transaction databases with a denominator of eligible firms would move the estimate.
s5.rationale: Succession and industry consolidation create control events, but many changes are internal, asset-only, or outside the retained custom-feeding lens.

principal_driver: Continuous animal and feed data can improve decisions and administration within a recurring custom-feeding relationship.
principal_weakness: Only part of the NAICS population is a qualifying outsourced service, and consolidation can preserve cattle volume while eroding independent operator availability.
